titleStmt.title: How Is Gender Portrayed on Preschool Children’s Book Covers? An Analysis of the Chinese National Library Catalogue between 2012-2022

author.affiliation: Te Shi Liangcai School of Journalism and Communication, Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, Hangzhou, China

keywords.term: Chinese children’s books; library catalogue; metadata; text mining; gender representation
I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

p.hi: The multimodality, especially the visual elements of picture books, empowers preschool children to read and perceive gender and other features independently
(Danko-McGhee & Slutsky, 2011; Jalongo, 2004)

. Research suggests preschool children are mostly illiterate, but they can recognise stories or books from covers and illustrations, they are even able to search for books in the libraries by describing the book cover
(Danko-McGhee & Slutsky, 2011; Kemple & Lopez, 2009; Li, 2022)
. Therefore, gender representations in these titles would influence how children see and think of gender by emphasising characters, narratives and storytelling
(Bal & Veltkamp, 2013; Bartholomaeus, 2016; Caple & Tian, 2022; Faver & Alanis, 2012)

. Library catalogues are filtered by librarians annually, and book selections are usually reviewed by experts in the children’s literature field. Library is a place to assist children’s right to search for information
(Brock & Rankin, 2012, p. 75; Busayo, 2011)
, library catalogues should be inclusive and cover different aspects of life, including gender. Book covers and titles, aside from the main body, are important parts of peritext
(Genette, 1997)
and can reveal the beginning of storytelling in preschool books
(Solomon Eaglstein & Berman, 1990)
. Previous studies examined book covers focus on their functions for books and diverse styles and designing
(Gruppetta, 2008; Osvath, 2021)

, but lack analysis from a gender perspective based on digital methods. To merge the gap, this study applies a combination of ChatGPT and text mining on the Chinese children’s library catalogues with a 10-year timeline (2012-2022).
We collected the children’s book catalogue of the National Library of China between 2012 and 2022
and selected books that target 0-6-year-old young readers as our research objects. We then searched ISBN for all titles on Douban (the largest reading community in China, equalise Goodreads)
, and crawled book covers of all 6,229 titles to make up the initial dataset using Python.
We first uploaded book covers of 6,229 titles one by one. In the initial stage, we asked ChatGPT
two questions for each title: 1) Is gender inferred in the book title? If so, what is it? 2) Are there any other elements suggesting gender? If so, what are they?

For each title, we will get two independent textual answers judging gender expressions from the book title and illustrations on book covers. Answers are semi-structured, suggesting if the title and illustrations infer gender or not, with further explaining the gendered word/phrase. For example, in the answer for a book title “Yes, gender is inferred in the book title 这是我爸爸 (
This Is My Dad
Hout, 2022)

. The term 爸爸 (dad) explicitly refers to a male parent, so the gender of the subject, "dad," is male”(ChatGPT 4.0, 2024), ChatGPT recognise the title includes a male character and identify the gender word “爸爸dad”. Similar answer structure are reported for the analysis of illustrations on book covers.

To check the precision of ChatGPT in gender detection, the two authors manually coded the first 60 books and compared them with ChatGPT answers. The Kappa coefficient for inter-rater reliability was 0.82, suggesting substantial agreement between coders.

To follow up, ChatGPT answers will make up a final corpus for our further analysis. We will use the term frequency to analyse the recurrences of gender/non-gender titles, classify the uses of different elements in illustrations to portray gender, and see how they vary from time (publication date) and book genre (CLCS
Figure 1
Research Process: Gender Detection on Children’s Library Catalogue of NLC
The results of this paper will be presented at the conference.

This study uses ChatGPT 4.0 to recognise gender embedded in preschool children’s books which are displayed in Chinese public libraries. It will reveal the semantic networks and associations between words to imply gender in children’s books. Based on the large language model, ChatGPT can exhibit gender perceptions for book covers from a public perspective, although it might also reflect gender biases
(Alexopoulos, Lyons, Mahetaji, Barnes, & Gutwillinger, 2023; Gross, 2023; Sebo, 2024)

and need to be improved. However, this will help librarians identify gender role assumptions and be aware of gender biases in categorising titles for future practice, thus improving gender equality and boosting diverse representations in the library collection of preschool children’s books.

div.note: The catalogue was published on the website of the National Library of China on 3/1/2024. Data can be publicly accessed at: http://kids.nlc.cn/srtsg/sytc/20240104_2637583.shtml

See https://www.douban.com to access Douban. Compared to WorldCat which is an open online platform and provides library archives and collections, Douban provides covers for Chinese books with high resolution.
ChatGPT supports Chinese and provides English analysis.
Chinese Library Classification System, see at https://www.clcindex.com

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Alexopoulos, M., Lyons, K., Mahetaji, K., Barnes, M. E., & Gutwillinger, R. (2023, November 24). Gender inference: Can chatGPT outperform common commercial tools? arXiv. doi: 10.48550/arXiv.2312.00805

Bal, P. M., & Veltkamp, M. (2013). How Does Fiction Reading Influence Empathy? An Experimental Investigation on the Role of Emotional Transportation. PLoS ONE, 8(1), e55341. doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0055341
Bartholomaeus, C. (2016). ‘Girls can like boy toys’: Junior primary school children’s understandings of feminist picture books. Gender & Education, 28(7), 935–950. doi: 10.1080/09540253.2015.1106443

Brock, A., & Rankin, C. (2012). Libraries, literacy and popular culture – let’s get reading! In A. Brock & C. Rankin (Eds.), Library Services for Children and Young People: Challenges and Opportunities in the Digital Age (pp. 65–88). Facet. doi: 10.29085/9781856048989.007

Busayo, I. O. (2011). The school library as a foundational step to childrens’ effective reading habits. Library Philosophy and Practice. Retrieved from https://go.gale.com/ps/i.do?p=AONE&sw=w&issn=15220222&v=2.1&it=r&id=GALE%7CA277107197&sid=googleScholar&linkaccess=abs

Caple, H., & Tian, P. (2022). Opening up semiotic spaces for gender expression: A case study of the construction of gender in Australian award-winning early childhood picture books. Visual Communication, 14703572211069604. doi: 10.1177/14703572211069604

Danko-McGhee, K., & Slutsky, R. (2011). Judging a book by its cover: Preschool children’s aesthetic preferences for picture books. International Journal of Education Through Art, 7(2), 171–185. doi: 10.1386/eta.7.2.171_1

Faver, C. A., & Alanis, E. (2012). Fostering empathy through stories: A pilot program for special needs adoptive families. Children and Youth Services Review, 34(4), 660–665. doi: 10.1016/j.childyouth.2011.12.012
Genette, G. (1997). Ebook of Paratexts: Thresholds of Interpretation. Cambridge University Press. Retrieved from https://hdl.handle.net/2027/heb40164.0001.001
Gross, N. (2023). What ChatGPT Tells Us about Gender: A Cautionary Tale about Performativity and Gender Biases in AI. Social Sciences, 12(8), NA-NA. doi: 10.3390/socsci12080435

Gruppetta, C. (2008). Getting them hooked: How can publishers entice children to pick up books and buy them? Start with the cover design, argues Chris Gruppetta, but make sure the jacket is flashy, the blurb exciting and the book has street cred. The Bookseller, (5357), 24–27.
Hout, M. V. (2022). This Is My Father (First Edition). Shao Nian Er Tong Chu Ban She.

Jalongo, M. (2004). Young Children and Picture Books. 2nd Edition. In National Association for the Education of Young Children. National Association for the Education of Young Children, 1509 16th Street, NW, Washington, DC 20036.
Kemple, K. M., & Lopez, M. (2009). Blue Eyes, Brown Eyes, Cornrows, and Curls: Building on Books to Explore Physical Diversity With Preschool Children. Dimensions of Early Childhood, 37(1), 23–30.
Li, S. (2022). Preschool Education Students’ Understanding of Children’s Picture Books. Wireless Communications and Mobile Computing, 2022, e9697874. doi: 10.1155/2022/9697874
Osvath, C. (2021). Uncovering Death: A Dialogic, Aesthetic Engagement with the Covers of Death-Themed Picture Books. Children’s Literature in Education, 52(1), 68–87. doi: 10.1007/s10583-020-09399-5
Sebo, P. (2024). What Is the Performance of ChatGPT in Determining the Gender of Individuals Based on Their First and Last Names? JMIR AI, 3, e53656. doi: 10.2196/53656
Solomon Eaglstein, A., & Berman, Y. (1990). You can tell a book by its cover: An analysis of blurbs. Aslib Proceedings, 42(1), 17–30. doi: 10.1108/eb051156
